question: Why can't you just use a plain map as a long-lived cache?
options: Maps are too slow to look up
A map grows without bound and eventually exhausts memory — you need a size cap and an eviction policy
Maps can't store strings
Maps lose data on every write
explanation: An unbounded map never removes entries, so it climbs until the process is OOM-killed. Bounding the size forces the real question: which entry to evict when it's full.

question: What does LRU use to decide what to evict, and why is that a good bet?
options: The largest value, to free the most memory
The least-recently-used item, because temporal locality makes recency a strong predictor of near-future use
A random item, for fairness
The most frequently used item
explanation: LRU evicts whatever was touched longest ago. Temporal locality says recently used items are likely to be used again, so the least-recently-used one is the safest to forget.

question: Why does an O(1) LRU need BOTH a hash map and a doubly linked list?
options: For redundancy in case one fails
The map gives O(1) key lookup but no order; the list gives recency order but O(n) search — combined (map points at list nodes) both lookup and eviction are O(1)
The list stores keys and the map stores values
To support concurrent readers
explanation: Neither structure alone does all of lookup, ordering, and O(1) eviction. The map finds the node instantly; the doubly linked list lets you unlink it and evict the tail in constant time.

question: Why must the recency list be DOUBLY linked, not singly linked?
options: To use more memory
To unlink a node in O(1) you need its previous neighbor — only a doubly linked node knows who precedes it
Singly linked lists can't grow
To store two values per node
explanation: Removing a node in constant time requires rewiring its predecessor's next pointer. A singly linked node doesn't know its predecessor, forcing an O(n) scan to find it.

question: A nightly analytics job scans every row once. What can this do to a pure-LRU cache serving live traffic?
options: Nothing — LRU is scan-resistant
Scan pollution: each cold key is touched once, marked recently-used, and evicts the hot set, collapsing the hit ratio
It doubles the hit ratio
It permanently corrupts the cache
explanation: A one-time sequential scan makes every cold key look 'recently used,' pushing out the genuinely hot items. Frequency-aware policies (TinyLFU/W-TinyLFU) defend against this by admission control.

question: In a plain LRU, why is even a read (Get) not safe for concurrent use without a lock?
options: Reads never touch shared state
Get performs a move-to-front, which mutates the shared list — so reads are effectively writes and must be synchronized
Go maps are always thread-safe
Only Put changes the cache
explanation: Get updates recency by moving the node to the front, mutating the list. That's why production caches shard the keyspace across many locks instead of serializing everything behind one.
